perturbation.original_text: In the event that we or any of our representatives receive a request or are required (by deposition, interrogatory, request for documents, subpoena, civil investigative demand or similar process) to disclose all or any part of the Evaluation Materials, we or our representatives, as the case may be, agree to (i) immediately notify the Company of the existence, terms and circumstances surrounding such a request, (ii) consult with the Company on the advisability of taking legally available steps to resist or narrow such request and (iii) assist the Company in seeking a protective order or other appropriate remedy.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that we or any of our representatives receive a request or are required (by deposition, interrogatory, request for documents, subpoena, civil investigative demand or similar process) to disclose all or any part of the Evaluation Materials, we or our representatives, as the case may be, agree to (i) notify the Company of the existence of such a request if practical, (ii) consider consulting with the Company on the advisability of taking steps to resist or narrow such request and (iii) possibly assist the Company in seeking a protective order or other appropriate remedy.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'immediately notify' to 'notify the Company of the existence of such a request if practical' weakens the notification requirement. Changing 'consult with the Company on the advisability of taking legally available steps to resist or narrow such request' to 'consider consulting with the Company on the advisability of taking steps to resist or narrow such request' weakens the consultation requirement. Finally, changing 'assist the Company in seeking a protective order or other appropriate remedy' to 'possibly assist the Company in seeking a protective order or other appropriate remedy' weakens the assistance requirement. These changes make the obligations less mandatory and more discretionary.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 3101
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 3101 governs the scope of disclosure in civil actions. While it allows for protective orders, it anticipates a good-faith effort to comply with discovery requests. By making the notification, consultation, and assistance obligations discretionary, the modified text could be interpreted as undermining the duty to cooperate in discovery, potentially leading to delays and disputes in litigation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules Law § 3101 (2022) - Scope of Disclosure. :: 2022 New York Laws :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the New York Laws 2024 N.Y. Laws 2023 N.Y. Laws 2022 N.Y. Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New York Laws CVP - Civil Practice Law and Rules Article 31 - Disclosure 3101 - Scope of Disclosure. Universal Citation: NY CPLR § 3101 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Next § 3101. Scope of disclosure. (a) Generally. There shall be full disclosure of all matter material and necessary in the prosecution or defense of an action, regardless of the burden of proof, by: (1) a party, or the officer, director, member, agent or employee of a party; (2) a person who possessed a cause of action or defense asserted in the action; (3) a person about to depart from the state, or without the state, or residing at a greater distance from the place of trial than one hundred miles, or so sick or infirm as to afford reasonable grounds of belief that he or she will not be able to attend the trial, or a person authorized to practice medicine, dentistry or podiatry who has provided medical, dental or podiatric care or diagnosis to the party demanding disclosure, or who has been retained by such party as an expert witness; and (4) any other person, upon notice stating the circumstances or reasons such disclosure is sought or required. (b) Privileged matter. Upon objection by a person entitled to assert the privilege, privileged matter shall not be obtainable. (c) Attorney's work product. The work product of an attorney shall not be obtainable. (d) Trial preparation. 1. Experts. (i) Upon request, each party shall identify each person whom the party expects to call as an expert witness at trial and shall disclose in reasonable detail the subject matter on which each expert is expected to testify, the subst
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original obligations regarding notification, consultation, and assistance, making them discretionary rather than mandatory. This undermines the duty to cooperate in discovery, which NY CPLR § 3101 emphasizes through 'full disclosure of all matter material and necessary'. By changing 'immediately notify' to 'notify...if practical,' 'consult' to 'consider consulting,' and 'assist' to 'possibly assist,' the modified contract introduces ambiguity that could be used to justify non-compliance with discovery requests, directly conflicting with the principle of full disclosure outlined in the law.

perturbation.original_text: Accordingly, unless required by applicable law, we agree that prior to the execution of a Sale Agreement with respect to the closing of a Possible Transaction, without the prior written consent of the Company, we will not, and we will direct our representatives not to, disclose to any person either the fact that discussions or negotiations are taking place concerning a possible transaction between us and the Company or any of the terms, conditions or other facts with respect to any such Possible Transaction, including the status thereof unless we are advised by counsel that we are required to make such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Accordingly, unless reasonably interpreted as required by applicable law, we agree that prior to the execution of a Sale Agreement with respect to the closing of a Possible Transaction, without what we deem to be the prior written consent of the Company, we will try not to, and we will suggest that our representatives not, disclose to any person either the fact that discussions or negotiations are taking place concerning a possible transaction between us and the Company or any of the terms, conditions or other facts with respect to any such Possible Transaction, including the status thereof unless we have a strong belief that we are required to make such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'reasonably interpreted' and 'what we deem to be' introduces subjectivity into the legal determination of requirements and consent, replacing 'we will not' with 'we will try not to' and 'direct our representatives not to' with 'suggest that our representatives not' weakens obligations. Replacing 'advised by counsel that we are required to make such disclosure' with 'have a strong belief that we are required to make such disclosure' weakens the reliance on legal counsel, allowing for personal judgment.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 prohibit manipulative and deceptive devices in securities trading, including the dissemination of false or misleading information. By weakening the non-disclosure obligations and introducing subjective interpretations, the modified text increases the risk of selective or misleading disclosure of information related to the possible transaction, which could be used for insider trading or other manipulative purposes. The original stricter language prevents this by mandating the parties not disclose any information unless required by counsel or the company.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The original contract language created a stricter non-disclosure agreement. The changed text introduces subjectivity, weakens the obligation to not disclose information, and reduces reliance on legal counsel. This creates ambiguity around the non-disclosure terms, making enforcement difficult. The Law Explanation states this increases the risk of selective or misleading disclosure, potentially leading to manipulative practices in securities trading, which Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 aims to prevent. Scraped Law Snippet 1 includes Section 10, supporting the relevance of the law. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a 'Document not Found' error, the information from Snippet 1, the Law Explanation and Perturbation explanation are sufficient to demonstrate how the changes introduce a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the Company's request, we shall promptly deliver to the Company or destroy all written Evaluation Materials and any other written materials without retaining, in whole or in part, any copies, extracts or other reproductions (whatever the form or storage medium) of such materials.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the Company's reasonable request, we shall endeavor to deliver to the Company or destroy most of the written Evaluation Materials and any other written materials without intentionally retaining, in whole or in part, any substantial copies, extracts or other reproductions (whatever the form or storage medium) of such materials.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'reasonable' before 'request' allows the recipient to dispute the validity of the request. Changing 'shall promptly deliver' to 'shall endeavor to deliver' weakens the obligation. Replacing 'all' with 'most of' creates ambiguity about what materials need to be returned. Replacing 'without retaining' to 'without intentionally retaining' allows for accidental retention and replacing 'any copies' with 'any substantial copies' allows for the retention of insubstantial copies.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) aims to protect trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to return or destroy evaluation materials and allowing for subjective interpretations and unintentional retention of information, the modified text increases the risk of misappropriation of trade secrets, potentially violating the DTSA. The original text, ensuring the prompt return and destruction of all documents, is far stronger and in compliance with the prevention of trade secrets theft.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens the obligation to protect trade secrets, creating ambiguity and increasing the risk of misappropriation, which contradicts the intent of the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), as explained in the Law Explanation. The original contract's strong language requiring the prompt return and destruction of all documents is weakened by the modified text, which introduces terms like 'reasonable request,' 'endeavor to deliver,' 'most of,' 'intentionally retaining,' and 'any substantial copies.' These modifications create loopholes that could allow for the unintentional or partial retention of sensitive information, undermining the protection of trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates civil proceedings can be initiated for violations related to trade secrets, which is relevant because the modifications increase the risk of such violations.
